common_name: Supersweet 100 Cherry Tomato (Classic Sweet Indeterminate Cherry)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Supersweet 100 is an indeterminate vine that produces prolifically throughout the season when grown with strong support (cage or stake 6+ feet tall). In zone 9, transplant late February through early March for harvest May through July, with a second planting in August–September for fall production. The long clusters (20–30+ tomatoes) must be harvested regularly; overripe fruits split easily.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun with strong support and consistent, even watering to prevent blossom end rot. Supersweet 100 is one of the most important cherry tomato varieties ever developed — a Petoseed hybrid released in the 1980s that set the standard for sweet cherry tomatoes and has remained one of the top-selling cherry tomatoes in America for over 40 years based solely on its exceptional eating quality. The name states the performance promise: 'super sweet' flavor from fruits with approximately twice the sugar content of beefsteak types, and '100' (now sold as 'Supersweet 100 F1') referencing a very early working name for the variety's productivity. The red, round, 1-inch fruits are produced in long, abundant clusters of 20–50+ tomatoes per cluster on indeterminate vines that require staking or caging and continue producing until frost. The flavor is memorably sweet, bright, and richly tomato-forward — thin-skinned enough to burst slightly on the palate, sweet enough to eat like candy, and balanced with just enough acidity to be complex rather than cloying. Supersweet 100 has also demonstrated excellent virus resistance for a variety of its era, which contributes to its continued commercial success. Every tomato gardener who grows cherry tomatoes should try Supersweet 100 as the benchmark against which other cherry varieties are compared.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Variety Evaluation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cherry Tomato Variety Comparison

common_name: Tumbling Tom Tomato (Cascading Container Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Tumbling Tom is specifically bred for hanging baskets, window boxes, and tumbling over the edges of large containers — the cascading, pendulous stems trail 18–24 inches and look spectacular draped over pot edges. Plant one per 12-inch hanging basket or 2–3 per large container. In zone 9, transplant in March–April for a May–June cherry tomato harvest from the patio or balcony.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into a hanging basket, window box, or large container with rich, well-draining potting mix. Water consistently (containers dry out faster than in-ground plantings) and fertilize every 2 weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer for best production. Tumbling Tom is a compact, pendulous-stemmed cherry tomato developed specifically for container and hanging basket growing — the first cherry tomato variety widely distributed with 'tumbling' in the name to describe its characteristic cascading habit, where the naturally trailing stems drape over pot edges and hang 18–24 inches below the rim, making it one of the most dramatic edible container plants available. Unlike most cherry tomatoes, which require staking and training on indeterminate vines that climb 5–8 feet, Tumbling Tom is a bushy, pendent type that requires no staking, no caging, and no training — just water, light, and fertilizer. The plants produce abundant clusters of small (1–1.5 inch), round, bright red cherry tomatoes with good sweet tomato flavor on the pendulous stems; a large hanging basket with a well-established Tumbling Tom plant loaded with red fruits is a genuinely beautiful and productive edible ornamental display. Available in both red and yellow strains ('Tumbling Tom Red' and 'Tumbling Tom Yellow'). Ideal for balconies, patios, decks, and any space where in-ground gardening is not possible.
sources: Thompson & Morgan — Tumbling Tom cherry tomato variety notes
Suttons Seeds — Container tomato growing guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato for Home Landscapes

common_name: Blondkopfchen Tomato (Little Blonde Girl German Heirloom Cherry)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Blondkopfchen is an indeterminate heirloom that requires strong staking or caging (6 feet minimum) and produces prolifically throughout the warm season. In zone 9, transplant late February–March for a May–July harvest. The extremely long clusters (30–50 small tomatoes per cluster) are one of the most visually remarkable features of this variety — a single plant in full production is spectacular.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun with a sturdy cage or tall stake. Blondkopfchen (German for 'little blonde girl' or 'little blondie') is a German heirloom cherry tomato that produces one of the most spectacular fruiting displays of any tomato variety — extremely long, drooping clusters of 30–50 or more tiny (0.5–0.75 inch), round, clear golden-yellow to light lemon-yellow fruits that cascade like bunches of golden grapes from indeterminate vines growing 6+ feet. The fruits are uniformly pale golden-yellow, translucent-skinned, and have an exceptionally sweet, mild, almost honey-sweet flavor with very low acidity — a delicate sweetness that is quite different from the richer red cherry types and makes Blondkopfchen appealing to those who find red cherry tomatoes too tart. The combination of the fruit's pale golden color, the translucent skin through which the interior is visible, and the long hanging clusters makes Blondkopfchen one of the most beautiful tomato plants in the garden — genuinely ornamental as well as productive and delicious. Very popular in European heirloom gardens and increasingly appreciated in the US by tomato collectors who value both visual character and eating quality. Seed Savers Exchange and specialty seed companies carry this variety as an example of German heirloom tomato excellence.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Blondkopfchen tomato variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — German heirloom tomato collection
Tomatoville (online heirloom tomato community) — Blondkopfchen discussions

common_name: Indigo Apple Tomato (OSU Indigo Series Blue-Anthocyanin Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Indigo Apple is an indeterminate variety that requires staking or caging. The distinctive blue-black anthocyanin pigmentation develops on sun-exposed areas of the fruit and fades on shaded portions, creating a characteristic bicolor appearance (blue-black on top, red on the shaded side). In zone 9, transplant February–March; the deep blue color develops most intensely in bright sun and cool temperatures.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun for maximum anthocyanin expression — the blue-black pigmentation only develops in sun-exposed areas and fades entirely in shade. Indigo Apple is a product of the Oregon State University tomato breeding program led by Dr. Jim Myers, who developed the 'Indigo' series of tomatoes by crossing commercial tomatoes with a wild species (Solanum cheesmaniae from the Galapagos Islands) that carries genes for anthocyanin production in fruit tissue. The anthocyanins (the same pigments in blueberries and purple cabbage) accumulate in the skin of Indigo tomatoes when exposed to sunlight, creating a striking blue-black to deep purple coloration on the sun side while the shaded side remains red — a unique bicolor effect that changes as the fruit ripens and is genuinely unlike any other common tomato. Indigo Apple produces medium-sized (3–4 oz) round fruits that hang in clusters; the flesh inside is red and has good complex tomato flavor with moderate sweetness and acidity. Beyond the visual novelty, the anthocyanin compounds are genuine antioxidants of nutritional significance — the same compounds studied for potential health benefits in blueberries. Indigo Apple, Indigo Rose, Indigo Blue Berries, and other Indigo series varieties represent a fascinating example of targeted nutritional breeding combined with ornamental appeal.
sources: Oregon State University Department of Horticulture — Indigo tomato breeding program
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Indigo Apple tomato variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Indigo series tomato collection

common_name: Black Plum Tomato (Russian Heirloom Dark-Red Paste Cherry)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Black Plum is an indeterminate heirloom that requires staking or caging and produces prolifically throughout the season. In zone 9, transplant February–March for May–July harvest. The distinctive dark, mahogany-red to near-black skin color intensifies with warm temperatures and full sun exposure; the rich flavor is best appreciated at room temperature rather than refrigerated.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun with strong cage or stake support and consistent, even moisture. Black Plum is a Russian or Eastern European heirloom paste-type cherry tomato that has become one of the most appreciated 'dark' tomato varieties in American heirloom tomato culture — producing small (1.5–2 inch), plum-shaped fruits with an extraordinarily dark skin color ranging from deep mahogany-red to near-black-purple on the sun-exposed surface and shoulders. The dark color comes from high levels of chlorophyll that breaks down to brownish-red tones in the skin, combined with the fruit's deep red flesh, creating an intensely rich, almost chocolatey appearance that makes Black Plum one of the most visually distinctive cherry/paste types. The flavor is equally distinctive: rich, complex, and deeply sweet with more winey, umami-forward notes than typical sweet red cherry types — a sophisticated flavor more commonly associated with large beefsteak types than small tomatoes. Black Plum produces in abundant clusters throughout the season and is excellent fresh in salads, halved on cheese plates, dried whole, or roasted — the meaty, paste-type interior with relatively few seeds makes it particularly good for drying and roasting. One of the most beautiful and flavorful of all dark tomato varieties.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Black Plum tomato variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Russian and Eastern European heirloom tomatoes
